title: AI triage crosses ER threshold as audit layer assembles - TCR 05/04/26
description:
A Harvard Medical School and Beth Israel study found OpenAI's o1 reached the correct ER triage diagnosis 67% of the time against attending physicians' 50-55%, Denmark's grid operator Energinet paused new data center connections after 60 GW of applications hit a 7 GW national peak demand, and a Cochrane meta-analysis of 17 trials and 20,342 participants found anti-amyloid Alzheimer's drugs produce no clinically meaningful benefit.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-may-4-2026/

0:00 Open and framing
5:22 Kenya Social Health Authority algorithm investigation
8:24 Denmark's fantasy queue and grid moratorium
10:19 ARIA disclosure and Downing Street FOI fight
13:22 Cochrane Alzheimer's verdict and capability decoupling

This episode covers the Harvard ER triage study and what it means for AI deployment frameworks, the Cochrane verdict overturning a generation of Alzheimer's drug assumptions, Denmark's grid moratorium and the "fantasy queue" framing from the industry's own association, the Kenya Social Health Authority algorithm investigation by Africa Uncensored and Lighthouse Reports, and the UK ARIA disclosure routing £50 million to US tech firms alongside the Downing Street meeting log obtained after a 12-month FOI fight.

Five domains, one structural pattern. The Harvard ER study moves the AI diagnostic capability question past the threshold where deployment is aspirational - the capability now exists for frontier reasoning models to outperform attending physicians at the moment information is most scarce. The same week, Cochrane's audit layer overturned a generation of Alzheimer's drug assumptions, decoupling diagnostic capability from therapeutic capability on the same week. Denmark's grid operator paused its own queue when applications hit nearly nine times national peak demand, and the industry's own association CEO called for new viability and societal-value criteria. An investigation into Kenya's healthcare algorithm revealed the system overcharged the poorest families on a design choice that proxy means testing across dozens of countries had been making implicitly for thirty years - made legible because algorithms produce auditable artifacts where paper systems did not. And in the UK, a 12-month FOI fight surfaced sixteen previously undisclosed meetings between a Downing Street adviser and Silicon Valley executives, while ARIA's £50 million flow to US tech firms drew parliamentary scrutiny. The verification layer is being assembled in public, asynchronously, by whoever moves first.
